title: SCMP analysis: Asia braces for oil-price shock from US-Iran confrontation
summary: South China Morning Post regional analysis on April 30 examines how Brent crude's spike to $126 per barrel and the ongoing US naval blockade of Iran threaten Asian energy importers, especially China, India, Japan and South Korea, who together account for the majority of pre-blockade Iranian crude offtake.
contents:
# SCMP analysis: Asia braces for oil-price shock from US-Iran confrontation

## Regional Impact Picture
South China Morning Post analysis on April 30 surveyed Asian government and industry responses to the spike in Brent crude to $126 per barrel and the continued US naval blockade of Iran. China, India, Japan and South Korea collectively absorbed the majority of Iranian crude exports prior to the blockade, with China accounting for the largest share. The newly designated Chinese refinery in OFAC's latest action signals a sharp escalation of secondary-sanctions exposure for Chinese downstream buyers.

## Beijing's Calculus
SCMP cites Chinese analysts assessing that Beijing faces difficult tradeoffs: continued covert purchases through dark-fleet routes risk new US designations, while compliance with the blockade hands Washington a strategic win. Indian refiners, in contrast, accelerated long-haul replacement sourcing from Latin America and West Africa. Japanese and South Korean refiners drew down strategic petroleum stocks while accelerating Saudi and UAE crude lifting.

## Strategic Reading
SCMP frames the Iran blockade as a stress test of Asian strategic autonomy, with implications for how Asian governments respond to future US economic-statecraft actions affecting other targets, including potential secondary measures relating to Russia. Brent eased slightly during the day after the initial $126 print.
